channel.title: Jerusalem International Fellows Cookbook on JIF Cookbook
channel.description: Recent content in Jerusalem International Fellows Cookbook on JIF Cookbook

item.title: Budget and Funding Framework
item.description: Budget and Funding Framework The program operates on a per-cohort basis. Cost models vary significantly by city, cohort size, and local cost of living. The 2022 program cost structure is documented below as a reference point; salary ranges, housing costs, and stipends will change by location and year. Direct Fellowship Costs Per-Fellow Cost Breakdown (2022 Jerusalem Reference Model) For a 10-week international residency, budget the following per Fellow: Airfare (round-trip, international): $2,000 Ground transportation (airport pickup, local taxis, tours): $300 Housing (12 weeks, shared apartment, utilities included): $7,500 Artist stipend, per diem, and materials: $8,500 Insurance (international travel and residency coverage): $500

item.title: For Replicators
item.description: For Replicators This folder contains operational manuals and frameworks for launching the program in a new city. Methodology - The five guiding questions and core philosophy Budgets - Scalable cost templates for three economic tiers Staffing - Organizational structure and recruitment timelines Host Organization Vetting - Partnership evaluation rubric Selection Process - Fellow recruitment and vetting lifecycle

item.title: Jerusalem International Fellows: Financial Materials Inventory
item.description: Jerusalem International Fellows: Financial Materials Inventory Drive Search Results | July 19, 2026 EXECUTIVE SUMMARY Drive search completed successfully. Recovered comprehensive financial planning documents spanning 2014-2026, including strategic projections, actual budgets (2014-2018), and a detailed 2026 replication model. Critical findings: 2014-2018 American Academy in Jerusalem (AAJ) complete budget plan located (scanned from physical document) 2026 financial model framework (complete) already drafted by Alexandra 2022 actual P&L remains MISSING Donor lists exist but are incomplete (foundation + individual donor names identified) No evidence of detailed expense receipts or 2022 final accounting IN HAND: DOCUMENTS RECOVERED 1.

item.title: Jerusalem International Fellows: Financial Operations Playbook
item.description: Jerusalem International Fellows: Financial Operations Playbook Version 1.0 | July 19, 2026 Status: Phase 1 (Structural) complete. Phase 2 (Reconciliation) built with verification flags for Elise data. Prepared by: Alexandra Nyashina, ANyashina LLC Prepared for: Elise Bernhardt, Jerusalem International Fellows DOCUMENT ARCHITECTURE This playbook captures the complete financial operations model for JIF replication. It is structured in two layers: PHASE 1: Structural models and frameworks (rates, FTE calculations, cost templates) — these are stable across iterations PHASE 2: Reconciliation and projections — flagged with what data is pending from Elise’s 2022 actuals All projections are built from the 2026 Financial Model Framework (Kyiv/Krakow/Chicago templates).

item.title: JIF Cookbook: Section 3 – The Recipes
item.description: JIF Cookbook: Section 3 – The Recipes How the Program Actually Runs The Selection Process: Building the Nominator Network The program does not post an open call. It works through a curated nominator network built over years. Timeline: Year 1 (nominator cultivation): Elise and advisory committee identify and cultivate 20-30 cultural leaders, curators, and presenters in relevant disciplines (visual art, performance, interdisciplinary practice, architecture, urban planning) who have demonstrated commitment to the work and credibility with artists.

item.title: Replication Manual: Budgets, Cash Flows, and Cost Templates
item.description: Replication Manual: Budgets, Cash Flows, and Cost Templates Scalable Operating Models by Cost-of-Living Context The replication framework provides detailed annual projections across three economic tiers, assuming a standard cohort of six fellows and six host organizations: 1. Kyiv Template (Low Cost-of-Living Baseline: $172,884 Total) Core Team Staffing ($112,000): Executive Director: $30,000 Program Director: $28,000 Communications Manager: $18,000 Finance (0.5 FTE): $6,000 Host Coordinators: $30,000 Direct Fellow Allocations ($31,468): Airfare: $5,400 Housing ($18/night): $9,072 Individual Meals: $6,048 Communal Salon Food: $3,048 Transport: $2,400 Materials: $1,500 Insurance: $4,000 Program Operations ($29,416):

item.title: Replication Manual: Fellow Recruitment and Selection Process
item.description: Replication Manual: Fellow Recruitment and Selection Process The Closed Nomination Engine The program rejects open calls to protect applicant quality and cohort temperament. Operations rely on a warm network of expert nominators who understand the cultural friction of the host city. The 18-Month Recruitment Lifecycle Months 1-3 (Nominator Cultivation): The Executive Director engages a pool of curators, presenters, program alumni, and educators across specific disciplines. The team distributes crisp selection parameters during 1:1 orientation calls.

item.title: Replication Manual: Host Organization Vetting and Partnership Rubric
item.description: Replication Manual: Host Organization Vetting and Partnership Rubric Partnership Principles Host organizations function as institutional co-creators and local anchors rather than passive venue providers. Replicators must select hosts that represent the diverse demographic and geographic realities of the city. Evaluation Rubric: Six Core Criteria Vetting teams must evaluate prospective hosts utilizing a formal six-part pass framework: Portal Capacity: The institution demonstrates verified expertise and active networks within the fellow’s artistic discipline.

item.title: Replication Manual: Staffing Models and Delegation Guidelines
item.description: Replication Manual: Staffing Models and Delegation Guidelines Core Organizational Architecture The program functions through a clear division of labor between strategic governance and local, on-ground execution. Replicators must fund five essential core components: [Executive Director] -- Strategy, Board Governance, Major Donors | [Program Director] -- Local Operations, Host Logistics, Fellow Support | [Communications Manager] -- Salon Curation, Multilingual PR, Translations | [Finance Administrator] (0.5 FTE) -- Budget Tracking, Invoicing, Tax Compliance | [Host Organization Coordinators] (Part-Time) -- On-Site Fellow Embedding Detailed Capability Requirements Executive Director (1.

item.title: Replication Manual: The Methodology of Serendipity
item.description: Replication Manual: The Methodology of Serendipity Core Philosophy The Jerusalem International Fellows framework functions as an active intervention in social and political deadlocks rather than a traditional cloistered artist retreat. The model uses the unique saturations of divided urban geographies to inspire contemporary creative practice. By embedding international creators within local institutional anchors, the program establishes portals of engagement that bypass polarized external narratives. The Five Guiding Questions for City Adaptation Replication teams must evaluate their local context by answering five structural questions:

item.title: Section 1: Timeline
item.description: Section 1: Timeline Program History, Cohort Changes, Structural Redesigns, Key Partnerships Status: COMPLETE (journalistic voice, comprehensive) Last Updated: June 23, 2022 (as part of 2022 evaluation) Overview This section provides a chronological record of the Jerusalem International Fellows program’s evolution from its founding through the 2022 pilot residency. It captures key milestones, structural changes, political shifts, and the decisions that shaped the direction of the work. Key Milestones 2010-2013: American Residencies (Foundation Era) The program began under the Federation for Jewish Community (FJC) umbrella as the American Academy in Jerusalem (AAJ).

item.title: Section 2: Kitchen Notes
item.description: Section 2: Kitchen Notes Voices from the 2022 Residency The following is synthesized from the On Track Consulting evaluation report (June 2022) and guest attendance documentation. Each voice speaks about their experience and what mattered most. From the Fellows Vibha Galhotra (Conceptual Artist, New Delhi) On the disorientation week: “The orientation included engaging tours to understand some of the layers and complexities that make Jerusalem unique. I have never experienced such a strong religious culture experience.

item.title: Section 3: The Recipes
item.description: Section 3: The Recipes How the Program Actually Runs The Selection Process: Building the Nominator Network The program does not post an open call. It works through a curated nominator network built over years. Timeline: Year 1 (nominator cultivation): Elise and advisory committee identify and cultivate 20-30 cultural leaders, curators, and presenters in relevant disciplines (visual art, performance, interdisciplinary practice, architecture, urban planning) who have demonstrated commitment to the work and credibility with artists.

item.title: Section 4: The Network / Ingredients
item.description: Section 4: The Network / Ingredients Who the People Are, How They Connect Core Local Relationships (Jerusalem-based) Ruth Cummings (Jerusalem Culture Unlimited, East Jerusalem Advisor) 12+ year relationship with Elise and JIF Primary bridge to East Jerusalem cultural community Provides credibility in non-normalization frameworks Key stakeholder feedback: “The Jerusalem Fellows program was a shot of B12 for every person who participated—artist, host, guests, viewers. It was a doppler effect of creativity radiating out into the city.

item.title: Section 5: The Vision
item.description: Section 5: The Vision The Why: Methodology of Serendipity What This Program Is Actually For Bringing together artists, the public, a complicated city, crazy politics, and impossible odds is what makes Elise tick and is the stuff of miracles. That sentence deserves unpacking because it contains the entire framework. The Jerusalem International Fellows is not a service program. It’s not filling a gap in artist funding (though that happens). It’s not providing a cultural exchange (though that happens too).

item.title: Supporting Materials Index
item.description: Supporting Materials Index This index lists all supporting documentation and replication assets for the Jerusalem International Fellows Cookbook website. Supporting Documents Title Description 2022 On Track Consulting Evaluation Report Independent assessment of the 2022 pilot residency cohort. Analyzes operational successes, participant feedback, and structural recommendations. Financial Operations Playbook Details organizational staffing cost structure, itemized fellow expenditures, and salon event economics. Provides baseline financial models to scale the residency across different city environments.
